summary: Ronacher argues that “90% AI-written code” is already his lived reality on a serious Go infrastructure service, not a distant prediction. He emphasizes that this only works because he enforces strong architectural standards and treats every AI-generated line as his responsibility. The post details how he structures projects (raw SQL, OpenAPI-first, PR-sized chunks) and how he pairs tools like Claude and Codex for complementary strengths. He warns that agents are error-prone, create inappropriate abstractions, and can hide failures unless tightly guided. His conclusion is that AI accelerates engineering and unlocks better trade-offs, but it never replaces the need for strong engineering judgment and accountability.
key_points: High AI code percentage is feasible in production, but only with strict human ownership of architecture, layout, and quality.
OpenAPI-first design and raw SQL become more attractive because AI removes the tedium while improving debuggability.
Agent workflows require deliberate scoping, iterative review, and context curation to avoid duplication and bad abstractions.
AI excels at rapid research-to-implementation, refactoring, infra setup, and SQL generation, shrinking weeks to days.
Without strong engineering judgment, AI yields brittle systems, poor runtime behavior, and opaque failure modes.
money_quotes: I still treat every line as my responsibility, judged as if I wrote it myself.
The fact that I no longer have to write SQL because the AI does it for me is a game changer.
I cannot stress enough how bad the code from these agents can be if you're not careful.
You constantly need to learn how to bring the right information to the context.
It is easy to create systems that appear to behave correctly but have unclear runtime behavior when relying on agents.
For me, this has reached the point where I can't imagine working any other way.
The tools are powerful, but they don't absolve you of responsibility.
key_insight: AI can now generate the bulk of production code, but only engineers with strong judgment and ownership can keep those systems reliable.
